what_it_does: Registers two MCP tools for the session fabric: a consolidated read tool `sessions` (with ops list/inbox/watch/describe/search/timeline) and a single write tool `session_post` that appends durable intent records to a mailbox for delivery, wake, consult, or queue routing.
observations: Exports a closed op set `OPS` for the consolidated read tool.
Uses `contracts.tools.ToolAnnotations` mapped to `mcp.types.ToolAnnotations` for SDK hints.
Implements CQRS: `sessions` is read-only; `session_post` is the only write.
Routes `session_post` verbs by target session state from the registry, with explicit refusal paths.
Supports point-in-time launches via `at` using compaction points, event UUIDs, or timestamps.
Mailbox bodies are stored via `cas://` and resolved through `suite.store.get_content`.
Includes extensive inline documentation and cross-references to guide sections and criteria audits.
standards: The `session_post` router encodes state-based routing rules explicitly in code rather than hiding them.
Point-in-time validation is performed at post-time before appending to the mailbox.
The `_registry_guard` helper produces teaching errors when registry seams are missing.
conventions: Follows a consolidated-tool pattern: one read tool with an `op` parameter instead of many flat tools.
Uses `session://<id>` address forms internally while accepting bare UUIDs.
Validates inputs with `ValueError` carrying explanatory teaching messages.
Reads environment at call time for tunable concurrency cap.
concerns: `sys.path.insert` mutation at module import time couples load order to directory depth.
Default concurrency cap of 3 is flagged as hardcoded, pending registry-served default.
`from_session` defaults to empty string but is required and raises if missing, which is slightly misleading in the signature.
Dynamic imports of `runtime.session_search` and `runtime.session_pointintime` inside functions make static dependency tracing harder.
notes: This is the agent-facing MCP tool layer for cross-session messaging, deliberately kept write-proof by only appending mailbox intents and leaving all process lifecycle actions to a separate supervisor service.
questions: What exactly does `suite.store.append_agent_mail` return, and what fields does it guarantee?
How are `agent_mail_since` filter semantics composed when multiple of `to`, `verb`, `thread`, and `since` are supplied?
What is the expected behavior when `op='watch'` is called with a `since` value beyond the current event log tail?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it exposes read-only session fabric queries and a single mailbox-append write tool, without directly spawning or resuming sessions.
apparent_themes: CQRS separation of reads and writes
State-based routing decisions rather than mechanism
Point-in-time session materialization without touching originals
intention_signals: The file is trying to make the session fabric safe and inspectable for agents: every cross-session interaction becomes a durable, auditable mailbox record, and agents must verify outcomes through reads rather than trusting the write response.
novelty: It is a relatively rare example of an MCP tool face that refuses to perform the consequential action itself (spawn/wake/inject) and instead delegates to a supervisor, exposing the resulting event stream and mailbox for client-side verification.
contribution: Provides the agent-facing API surface for session-to-session communication, including registry queries, mailbox reads, live event watching, content search handles, timeline-based launch points, and the single intent-posting write path.
summary_for_embedding: `company/mcp_face/tools/sessions.py` registers the `sessions` read tool and `session_post` write tool for the agent session fabric, enabling registry queries, mailbox reads, event watching, transcript search, timeline inspection, and durable intent posting routed by session liveness.
intention_for_embedding: To give agents a safe, auditable, CQRS-style interface for messaging and orchestrating other Claude Code sessions without directly manipulating their processes.

symbols.does: Returns the fabric concurrency cap used to limit consult fan-out.
symbols.description: Reads `COMPANY_FABRIC_CONCURRENCY` from the environment at call time, falling back to a hardcoded 3, and documents the intent to later source the default from registry-served config.

symbols.does: Maps the internal `ToolAnnotations` contract to the SDK's annotation hints.
symbols.description: Converts a `CompanyToolAnnotations` instance into an `SDKToolAnnotations` object, carrying readonly/destructive/idempotent hints and an optional `posture` security tag for remote gateway exposure.

symbols.does: Normalizes a session reference to a bare UUID.
symbols.description: Accepts either a bare session id or a `session://<id>` address, strips the prefix, and raises a teaching `ValueError` for empty or non-string input.

symbols.does: Wraps a bare session id in its `session://` address form.
symbols.description: Simple helper that produces the canonical session address string used as mailbox `to`/`from` values.

symbols.does: Raises a teaching error if a required Suite registry seam is missing.
symbols.description: Checks whether `suite.<what>` is callable; if not, it fails loudly explaining which registry lane is absent and which ops remain available without it.
Top-level registration entry point that wires the read and write tool definitions into the provided `mcp` instance, returning the two tool functions.

symbols.does: Implements the consolidated read tool for the session fabric.
symbols.description: Handles `list`, `inbox`, `watch`, `describe`, `search`, and `timeline` ops by querying the registry, mailbox, event store, search index, and compaction timeline; it never writes.

symbols.does: Implements the single write tool that appends a session intent to the mailbox.
symbols.description: Validates the target, message, verb, copies, and optional point-in-time, resolves the effective routing verb based on registry state, stores the message body via CAS, and appends a durable mail record for the supervisor to act on.
